era_context: The Roman Empire under Gordian III (238–244 CE) following the tumultuous Year of the Six Emperors. The Sassanid Persian Empire under Shapur I was consolidating power in the Near East. In China, the Eastern Wu dynasty controlled the south while the Wei dynasty dominated the north during the Three Kingdoms period. This was an era of military pressure on Rome's eastern frontier and internal consolidation in the East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Roman frontier (archaeological record thin)
Mesoamerica (no contemporary written records in European tradition)

events.title: Gordian III assumes sole power in Rome
events.description: Following the assassination of Balbinus and Pupienus in 238 CE, the young Gordian III (born 225 CE) became the undisputed emperor of Rome at approximately age 13–15. He was supported by the praetorian prefect Timesitheus, who effectively governed during his early reign. This marked the end of the chaotic Year of the Six Emperors and the beginning of a period of relative stability, though Gordian remained a figurehead during his early years.
events.certainty_note: Numismatic and literary sources confirm Gordian's reign from 238 CE onward. The exact date of assumption of sole power is conventional; sources agree on 238 CE as the transition year.

events.title: Shapur I consolidates Sassanid control in Mesopotamia
events.description: Shapur I (r. 240–270 CE) continued his military campaigns to secure Sassanid dominance over Mesopotamia and the Levant, challenging Roman control of the region. This period saw intensified conflict along the Euphrates frontier. Shapur's reign marked the beginning of sustained Sassanid pressure that would define the next three decades of Roman-Persian relations.

sources.name: Res Gestae Divi Saporis (Shapur's own inscription at Naqsh-i Rustam)

events.certainty_note: Shapur I's reign is well-attested from his own inscriptions. The specific dating of campaigns within his reign is sometimes approximate; 240 CE marks the conventional beginning of his active military phase.

events.title: Eastern Wu consolidates control in southern China
events.description: Under Sun Quan (r. 229–252 CE), the Eastern Wu kingdom continued to consolidate its control over the Yangtze River valley and southern coastal regions. By 240 CE, Wu had established itself as a major power in the Three Kingdoms period, with a developed administrative system and naval capabilities. This period saw economic development in the south and cultural flourishing distinct from the northern Wei state.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou

events.certainty_note: Eastern Wu's political consolidation is well-documented in Chinese historical records. Sun Quan's reign is securely dated.

events.title: Wei dynasty under Cao Rui maintains northern Chinese dominance
events.description: The Cao Wei state under Emperor Cao Rui (r. 226–239 CE) had recently ended; however, the Wei dynasty continued under regency and remained the dominant power in northern China. By 240 CE, the Wei state was experiencing internal political tensions following Cao Rui's death, with power concentrated among the Sima family, who would eventually usurp the throne. This period marked the beginning of the decline of Cao family authority.
events.certainty_note: Wei dynasty succession and regency are well-documented in Chinese historical records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Historia Augusta, a primary source for Gordian III's reign, is known to contain fabrications and legendary material, particularly regarding the early years of his reign. Modern scholarship treats its accounts with caution. The exact nature and extent of Timesitheus's influence versus Gordian's personal agency remains debated. Some sources attribute greater autonomy to the young emperor than others.
historiographic_note: The year 240 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire and China, with contemporary numismatic and epigraphic evidence supplementing later chronicles. The Historia Augusta is problematic but remains a primary source. Chinese sources are more reliable for Eastern Wu and Wei. Sassanid sources are limited to royal inscriptions, which present a propagandistic view. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from contemporary documentation.

graph_edges.note: Gordian III's sole power resulted from the resolution of the Year of Six Emperors
Gordian III's reign led to his Mesopotamian campaign and death in 244 CE
Shapur I's consolidation occurred during Gordian III's reign, creating frontier tensions
Eastern Wu and Wei were contemporary rivals in the Three Kingdoms period
